contact.label: GET IN TOUCH
contact.title: Let's work together?
contact.body: If you want to collaborate on a product, discuss an opportunity, or simply ask a question, feel free to reach out. I'm always open to new ideas, teams and collaborations where design and code coexist well.

case_study_futbol-talent-pro.context: FutbolTalent.Pro is an Argentine startup seeking to democratize access to professional football by directly connecting young players (ages 10-20) with scouts and clubs. The problem: talent in small cities or remote provinces stays off recruiters' radar, who rely on closed networks and personal recommendations. The platform proposes a mobile-first model where players can showcase their performance through statistics, videos, and achievements, while scouts and clubs discover talent quickly and measurably.
case_study_futbol-talent-pro.role: I worked as a UX/UI Designer ad honorem for 3 months (August-October 2025) under a confidentiality agreement. The team was small: myself, a Flutter developer, and the founders. My responsibility was to design the entire MVP experience: from initial wireframes to 40 high-fidelity screens for three distinct user profiles (player, scout, club), create the complete design system with light/dark mode, and coordinate with the developer to ensure technical feasibility during the implementation process.
case_study_futbol-talent-pro.process:
The main challenge was designing a freemium model that balanced democratic access with economic sustainability. The decision: players could create a free profile and apply to a limited number of calls, but the premium plan unlocked unlimited applications and full access to training courses. Scouts had the same scheme for signing players. This structure generated internal friction — the founders wanted to maximize conversion to paid plans, but I argued that the free tier needed to be functional enough to validate the product with real users before asking for money.

Another complex decision was the player explorer. The founders wanted an exhaustive filter system (position, age, region, statistics, achievements, ranking) that became infinite — almost a Google inside the app. I proposed a progressive approach: simple search by default (name, position, region) with an 'Advanced filters' button for power users. This reduced initial cognitive load and avoided cluttered screens.

For gamification, I designed a weekly ranking system, badges for achievements (first goal uploaded, 10 videos published, top scout of the month), and themed challenges that incentivized constant participation. The challenge was maintaining visual clarity — each gamification element had to be recognizable without saturating the interface. I created a design system with consistent iconography (⚽ for players, 🏆 for achievements, 🔥 for ranking) that worked in both light and dark modes.
case_study_futbol-talent-pro.solution:
I delivered 40 high-fidelity screens organized into three complete user flows: player (registration, profile creation, application to calls, viewing scout feedback), scout (player exploration, advanced filtering, marking interest, managing signed players), and club (posting calls, reviewing applicants, metrics analysis). The design system included a color palette with light/dark support, typographic system, reusable components (cards, buttons, inputs, badges), and spacing guides.

What I can't show due to NDA: final implemented screens and specific brand decisions. What I can share: low/medium-fidelity wireframes, flow diagrams, user personas, and the reasoning behind each information architecture decision.
case_study_futbol-talent-pro.results:
The project doesn't have user metrics yet (the app is in development), but it does have business validation: the founders presented the complete design to potential investors and received approval to continue with development. The Flutter developer implemented screens in parallel with my design work, which accelerated time-to-market and validated that all proposed interactions were technically viable.

Key learning: working under NDA forced me to exhaustively document design decisions from the start, knowing I couldn't show the final product. This reinforced my ability to articulate the 'why' behind each decision, not just the 'what' was designed.

case_study_el-ritual-del-tono.context:
Guitarists trying to replicate their favorite artists' sound face a problem: information is scattered and contradictory. You watch 10 YouTube videos about 'David Gilmour's tone' and each one tells you something different. Forums have technical discussions but are hard to navigate. Equipboard shows you what each artist uses, but doesn't tell you how to configure it for a specific song.

El Ritual del Tono solves this with a reverse search engine: you search by artist and song, and the platform gives you the complete signal chain (guitar → pedals → amplifier) needed to achieve that exact sound. It's not a generic gear store — it's a sonic discovery tool that also lets you buy the entire setup in one click.
case_study_el-ritual-del-tono.role: Personal full-stack project developed end-to-end as a technical demonstration of the MERN stack (MongoDB, Express, React, Node.js). I worked alone: from data modeling in MongoDB to interface design in React with Next.js and Tailwind CSS. The goal was to build a complete application that solved a real problem in the guitar world — a niche I know well — while demonstrating skills in frontend, backend, and NoSQL database architecture.
case_study_el-ritual-del-tono.process:
The hardest technical decision was the 'One-Click Setup': allowing a user to add all components of a sound (5-8 different gears) to the cart with a single button, without duplicating products, respecting stock, and keeping state synchronized between backend and Context API. I solved this with a dedicated endpoint that receives an array of IDs, validates availability in MongoDB, and returns only the addable items. The frontend receives the response and updates the cart atomically.

Another complex decision was gear reusability between artists. David Gilmour and John Mayer use similar pedals — if I modeled each gear as unique per artist, the database would explode. The solution: a shared Gears collection where each Song has an array of ObjectIds pointing to reusable products. This drastically reduced content load and kept data consistent.

To keep the project viable, I cut the initial scope: instead of 50 artists with 10 tones each, I focused on 13 iconic artists (Gilmour, Mayer, Frusciante, Cerati) with 2 tones each. This allowed me to finish a functional MVP without getting stuck curating content infinitely. I also simplified checkout: instead of integrating Stripe or MercadoPago, the flow persists the order in MongoDB and simulates confirmation — enough to demonstrate system transactionality.
case_study_el-ritual-del-tono.solution:
The final architecture: Node.js backend with Express exposing a REST API, MongoDB as database with three main collections (Artists with Songs as subdocuments, Gears as independent collection, Orders for checkout), and Next.js frontend with React Context API for cart management. Main flow: user enters → searches an artist → selects a song → sees the complete signal chain with tone description and embedded YouTube video → can add gears individually or the entire setup with one click → simulated checkout that persists the order.

The interface uses Tailwind CSS for a clean design that prioritizes content: artist pages highlight songs, song pages show the video and tone description first, and only then the necessary equipment. The cart is persistent during the session and calculates subtotals automatically.
case_study_el-ritual-del-tono.results:
The project is deployed on Vercel and works end-to-end: you can browse artists, explore tones, add products to cart and complete simulated checkout. It doesn't have real users yet — it's a technical demonstration — but it fulfilled its objective: validate that I can build a complete full-stack application from scratch.

Key learning: modeling data in MongoDB is fundamentally different from SQL. The decision to use subdocuments (Songs inside Artists) vs separate collections has huge implications for performance and query complexity. I chose subdocuments because 90% of queries are 'give me all songs from this artist', which makes populate unnecessary and speeds up responses. If I did it again, I would add authentication to let users create their own setups and share them with the community — turn the platform from personal curation to a social network for guitarists.

case_study_cabify-music-match.context:
Rides in mobility apps have an invisible problem: auditory friction. Either there's an awkward silence for 15 minutes, or music plays that the passenger didn't choose and doesn't like, but doesn't dare ask to change. Asking for the aux cable is awkward. Asking the driver to change the radio station too. Most people just put on headphones and disconnect.

Cabify has a music option, but it only works for changing radio stations — a technology almost nobody uses anymore. The real problem isn't technical, it's social: how do you align the musical tastes of two people who just met, without friction in the ride-requesting flow?
case_study_cabify-music-match.role: UX/UI design project developed individually over 3 months as a product thinking exercise. My role covered UX research, information architecture, flow design, wireframing, high-fidelity visual design, and interactive prototyping in Figma. The goal was to design a feature that solved a real pain point — ride discomfort — using existing technology (Spotify APIs) non-invasively.
case_study_cabify-music-match.process:
The hardest design decision was balancing the musical tastes of driver and passenger so both are satisfied without either feeling they lost control. Three possible paths: (a) prioritize the passenger (they're paying), (b) prioritize the driver (it's their car), or (c) intelligently merge tastes. I chose option (c) but with clear fallbacks.

The merge algorithm detects matches in genres and artists between both Spotify profiles and generates a temporary ride playlist. If there aren't enough matches, it offers pre-curated playlists by mood (energizing, relaxing, neutral) that statistically work for most people. And there's always the 'Silence' option for those who prefer not to listen to anything — validating this need was key, not everyone wants music.

Another challenge: onboarding. Linking Spotify can't disrupt the ride-requesting flow. The solution: make linking optional and progressive. The first time a user opens the app after the feature launch, they see a skippable explanatory modal. If they skip it, Music Match simply doesn't appear in their rides. If they link, the feature activates automatically in future rides with a simple toggle to disable it if they don't want to use it that day.

I validated the design with navigation maps and user flows tested with real users (drivers and passengers). The most valuable feedback: most didn't know Cabify already had a music option (the radio station selector), which confirmed the existing feature was poorly designed. I added a visible music icon on the ride start screen so the functionality is discoverable without relying on hidden menus.
case_study_cabify-music-match.solution:
Music Match integrates as a non-invasive feature within Cabify's existing flow. Before the ride: the user links Spotify once from settings (optional). During the ride: when confirming the driver, the app automatically generates the merged playlist and shows it on screen with a preview of 3-4 songs that will play. The passenger can accept it, change it to a pre-curated playlist, or choose silence. The driver sees the same interface and can make overrides if necessary.

The functional Figma prototype (iPhone 14) includes: skippable onboarding screen, Spotify linking flow with simulated OAuth, main ride screen showing the generated playlist with basic playback controls, and settings where you can unlink the account or adjust genre preferences. The entire design respects Cabify's visual system (colors, typography, components) to feel like a native extension, not a tacked-on feature.
case_study_cabify-music-match.results:
The project wasn't implemented — it's a speculative concept — but it validated my ability to detect real friction in existing products and propose technically viable solutions. The prototype is published on Behance and worked as a conversation tool in interviews: it demonstrates systems thinking (I didn't just design screens, I designed the merge algorithm and edge cases), user empathy (silence mode came from research, not my assumption), and implementation awareness (using existing Spotify APIs instead of inventing impossible technology).

Key learning: the best features aren't the most complex, they're the ones that solve emotional problems (the discomfort of silence) with simple technical solutions (connecting two Spotify accounts). If I did it again, I would design a post-ride feedback system for users to rate whether the playlist worked — that data would allow iteratively improving the merge algorithm and turn Music Match into a real competitive differentiator for Cabify.

case_study_multibrand-design-system.context:
EdTech companies with multiple products face a problem: each team designs independently, creating visual inconsistencies that confuse students and teachers. A button works differently on each platform. Colors change. Navigation isn't predictable. This not only damages the brand, but increases user cognitive load — critical in educational environments where clarity is fundamental.

No Country's brief was clear: design a multibrand Design System that would allow multiple educational products to share a common base (components, tokens, UX patterns) while each brand maintains its visual identity. The rule: 70% mandatory core, 30% customizable. Five weeks, team of 6 people distributed across Argentina, Chile and Colombia, 100% remote.
case_study_multibrand-design-system.role: I worked as a UX/UI Designer in a multicultural team of 6 people during a No Country work simulation (November 2025). The team included UX Researchers, other UX/UI designers, and we coordinated via Trello, Notion, FigJam and Google Meet. My main responsibility: design the Figma component library applying the tokens defined by the team, document usage patterns, and contribute to the UX Writing system for accessibility. I collaborated in defining the system architecture (tokens → foundations → components → patterns) and creating applied examples to validate that the system worked with different brands.
case_study_multibrand-design-system.process:
The hardest decision was defining which elements went into the 70% core (immutable for all brands) vs the 30% customizable. Each brand wanted creative freedom, but too much personalization breaks educational consistency. We solved this with research: we analyzed real EdTech platforms (Coursera, edX, Udemy, Platzi), identified common patterns, and created user personas — not of students, but of the designers and developers who would use the system.

The core includes: grid, spacing, radii, shadows, component behavior, AA accessibility rules, and UX patterns (onboarding, error feedback, learning progress). Customizable: primary/secondary/accent color, one typography (chosen from 3 pre-approved options: Inter, Rubik, Noto Serif), and iconography/illustrations with clear style rules.

Another complexity: coordinating 6 people in different time zones (GMT-3 to GMT-5). We implemented async dailies in Notion, design reviews twice a week on Meet, and a strict versioning system in Figma to avoid overwriting each other's work. I learned that clear documentation is more important than beautiful design — if a component isn't well documented (purpose, variants, states, Do's & Don'ts), nobody uses it correctly.

UX Writing was an unexpected challenge. We designed complete voice and tone guidelines for educational platforms: clear, human, motivating without being exaggerated, guiding without imposing. We created standardized microcopys for system states ("Loading...", "Saved", "Check your data"), empathetic error messages, and a brand adaptation system that allows adjusting tone without changing fundamental voice.
case_study_multibrand-design-system.solution:
We delivered a complete Design System with five main components: (1) Token architecture (color, typography, spacing, radii, shadows, motion) implemented in Figma Variables with light/dark mode support. (2) UI component library (buttons, inputs, cards, navigation, modals, alerts, tables, progress bars) with all documented variants and states. (3) EdTech-specific UX patterns (onboarding, educational forms, progress feedback, gamification, error handling). (4) Governance system defining roles (Owner, Maintainer, Contributors), approval flow for new brands, and exception request process. (5) Centralized Notion documentation with guides for non-technical teams, accessibility checklist, and step-by-step sub-brand creation process.

We validated the system by creating two fictional brands with opposite personalities (one formal/academic, another dynamic/innovative) applying only customizable tokens. We confirmed that 70% of components were reused without changes and both brands maintained visual coherence despite having different colors and typographies.
case_study_multibrand-design-system.results:
The project wasn't implemented in production — it was an academic simulation — but it fulfilled its objective: demonstrate ability to work in remote multidisciplinary teams under time pressure. We presented the complete system to No Country evaluators with a pitch deck explaining the problem, solution, and expected impact (reduced time-to-market, greater consistency, better student engagement).

Key learning: designing a Design System is 30% visual design and 70% governance and documentation. Beautiful components are useless if nobody knows when to use them or how to customize them without breaking the system. Multicultural collaboration taught me to communicate design decisions clearly and justified — you can't say "I like it better this way", you have to explain why that decision improves accessibility, consistency, or scalability. If I did it again, I would prioritize fewer components but better documented — we tried to cover too much in 5 weeks and some patterns were left without enough examples.

case_study_recuerdalo.context:
Older adults (70+) face an invisible barrier with technology: it's not that they can't use it, it's that current design assumes digital fluency they don't have. Health apps, reminders, online procedures — everything is designed for people who grew up with smartphones. The result: frustration, abandonment, and dependence on family members for tasks they should be able to do alone.

Recuérdalo was born as a university project (UX Research Techniques, 3 months) with a clear objective: design a reminders, cognitive stimuli and family support app specifically for older adults, based on real research with users in the target age range — not on assumptions about what they 'need'.
case_study_recuerdalo.role: I worked alone throughout the project. My responsibility covered complete research (semi-structured interviews, shadowing, open card sorting), qualitative analysis with affinity mapping, information architecture design, creation of user personas and journey maps, wireframing, high-fidelity visual design, and functional prototyping in Figma. The focus was on rigorous UX methodology: every design decision had to be backed by research evidence, not intuition.
case_study_recuerdalo.process:
The hardest design decision was balancing autonomy vs help. Older adults want to feel capable — personal pride in overcoming challenges is huge — but they also need support when they get stuck. Designing too much help feels condescending. Designing too little generates abandonment.

The key insight came from Carlos (80 years old) in an interview: 'They explain everything, I start well, but if I make a mistake in the third step, I don't know how to go back. I end up closing everything and starting over, and then I'm already tired.' This revealed that the problem isn't initial complexity, but sequential memory — forgetting where they were in a multi-step process.

The solution: design flows with visible reversible steps. Each action has a clear numerical indicator (Step 1 of 3), large 'Go back' buttons on each screen, and immediate visual confirmations (a large green check when they complete a task) that work as emotional micro-rewards. This transforms obligations (taking pills) into small daily achievements.

I conducted open card sorting with three older users using 15 cards of potential functionalities. The result: they grouped by emotional context, not by function type. Four categories emerged: 'My Obligations and Health' (pills, appointments, shopping list), 'My People' (contacts, family photos, calls), 'To Entertain and Think' (memory games, news, radio), and 'Important Things and Help' (passwords, ID, app help). The 'Help' category was revealing — they associated it directly with managing sensitive information, indicating they perceive those tasks as the riskiest and where they need the most support.
case_study_recuerdalo.solution:
The final Figma prototype implements the information architecture from the card sorting: four main sections accessible from a home menu with large icons and clear text labels. Each critical flow (schedule reminder, view medical appointments, add emergency contact) uses the reversible steps system with numerical indicators, visible 'Back' buttons, and visual confirmations.

Accessibility decisions: minimum 18px typography (vs 14px standard), AA contrast on all elements, minimum 60x60px touch buttons (vs 44px general recommendation) for less precise fingers, and a floating 'Help' button always visible that displays contextual tutorials for the current step or allows calling an emergency contact.

The micro-rewards system (large green check when completing tasks) came from prototype testing: users reported satisfaction seeing immediate visual confirmation, reducing the anxiety of 'did I do it right?'. This emotional detail is as important as technical functionality.
case_study_recuerdalo.results:
The project was presented at university as a complete case study: documented research (recordings, transcriptions, affinity diagrams), user personas with real quotes from interviewees, Raquel's journey map navigating an online procedure, low/medium fidelity wireframes, and functional Figma prototype with all main screens.

Key learning: inclusive design is not an aesthetic 'extra layer' you add at the end — it's the foundation of good experience. By designing for Carlos, Raquel and Laura, with their visual limitations, technological fears and fragile sequential memory, I ended up creating a clearer, more robust and easier-to-use product for anyone. Inclusion principles (clarity, reversibility, visual confirmation, contextual help) improve everyone's experience, not just the target audience.

If I did it again, I would expand the concept with a 'Family Peace of Mind System': link the app with the user's children so they receive automatic notifications when their parents take medication or attend an appointment. This would reduce family anxiety and reinforce the older adult's support network — turning the app into a generational bridge, not just an individual tool.

about.title: About me
about.p1: I'm a senior student in my final year of the Multimedial Technology degree at Universidad Maimónides. My journey began in digital communication, where I spent almost three years managing content and social media for Hotel Real Cafayate. That experience taught me to understand audiences and think strategically, but it also made it clear that I wanted to be on the other side: building the platforms, not just using them.
about.p2: Studying Multimedial Technology allowed me to connect design and code naturally. As a Teaching Assistant in Interface Design, I reinforced my ability to communicate complex concepts of usability and information architecture. Then came professional roles like Jr. Web Designer at Qualita Studio and projects like FutbolTalentPro, where I designed the MVP for a mobile-first app collaborating directly with developers. Those experiences confirmed that my hybrid approach works: I don't just design interfaces, I prototype them in Figma, validate them with users and code them in React.
about.p3: I'm looking to join product teams that value both design vision and the technical ability to execute it. My goal is to bridge the gap between UX and development, facilitating hand-off and ensuring that what gets designed is technically viable from the start.
